interpretation_summary: The replication attempt closely followed the preregistered plan: a Python translation of the original Stata mixed-effects test (cont ~ losses with random intercepts for sessioncode and id) was created and the included dataset was inspected. The data contain the required variables (cont, losses, id, sessioncode, loss_aversion, fear) and a much larger sample (1280 rows, ~320 participants) than the original Study 1 (N~62). However, execution failed to produce the planned model output: repeated attempts to run the Python script in the container and locally encountered file/path and script-syntax errors, and the analysis did not produce the expected results file (mccarter_mixed_results.txt). Therefore the attempt completed preparatory steps and diagnostics but did not yield final model estimates to compare with the original numerical claim.

fidelity_assessment.method_alignment: High alignment in intended methods: the agent implemented the same mixed-effects model structure as in the Stata .do (mixed cont losses || sessioncode: || id:) and planned the same exploratory checks (loss_aversion, fear). The dataset used contains the same key variables and the Python script used statsmodels.MixedLM to approximate the Stata mixed model. Major deviation is that the provided dataset is a larger, combined/extended sample (Prolific/Qualtrics/lab modes) rather than the original small undergraduate lab sample.

deviations.issue_description: Dataset differs from the original study sample (much larger, mixed collection modes).
deviations.impact: Medium-High: The larger / mixed-mode sample means any successful estimates would be a conceptual/robustness replication rather than a direct reanalysis of the original small undergraduate lab sample; effect sizes and SEs may differ for that reason.

deviations.issue_description: Code / execution problems: Python script had path/IO assumptions pointing to '/app/data/...' and contained accidental duplicated fragments during edits, producing syntax errors and FileNotFoundErrors when executed locally; container execution also failed to find the entry path at runtime.
deviations.impact: High: These errors prevented the mixed-effects models from running and therefore blocked production of the planned replication estimates.

deviations.issue_description: Output file path (/app/data/mccarter_mixed_results.txt) did not exist or was not writable in the execution environment when run locally, and orchestrator failed to execute the entrypoint inside the container due to path/mount mismatches.
deviations.impact: High: Prevented writing/collecting model summaries even when parts of script ran.

results_comparison.hypothesis_tested: Keeping expected returns constant, contributions ('cont') will be lower in rounds where the outcome distribution includes loss prospects (losses == 1) than in rounds without loss prospects (losses == 0).
results_comparison.original_results: Original Study 1 reported mean contributions: no-variance ~66.52 vs losses-and-gains ~58.98 (points out of 125) with F(1,61) = 4.73, p < .05, i.e., a significant negative effect of loss prospects on contributions (authors' claim: contributions were significantly lower under losses-and-gains).
results_comparison.replication_results: No finalized model estimates were produced by the executed pipeline. The replication team inspected the dataset and computed descriptive summaries (cont variable present, distribution summary shown), but mixed-effects model output (coefficient, SE, p-value for 'losses') was not generated due to execution errors. No mccarter_mixed_results.txt or comparable model output was produced in the study folder.
results_comparison.overall_answer: No — the replication did not produce the confirmatory model estimates so it cannot be judged against the preregistered comparison criteria. Preparatory and diagnostic steps succeeded, but code/execution failures prevented obtaining replication statistics.

replication_report: Incomplete replication: the analysis plan and Python translation matched the preregistered mixed-effects approach and the provided CSV contains the needed variables, but execution failed (syntax/path/IO errors and container entrypoint issues) and no model estimates were produced to determine whether contributions were lower under loss prospects.

failure_handling.suggestions: 1) Fix the Python script: remove duplicated/concatenated fragments produced during edits; ensure DATA_PATH resolution logic is robust and not duplicated. 2) Use safe file names or escape spaces; or better, use os.path.join and a filename without spaces (rename CSV to remove spaces) so path issues are avoided. 3) Write output files to a relative path inside the repository (e.g., './replication_data/mccarter_mixed_results.txt') in addition to /app/data to ensure outputs are available on host. 4) Test the script locally using the absolute host path to the CSV (or update the script to try local-relative paths first).

1) Ensure replication_info.json entrypoint is a simple relative script (e.g., './run.py') and that the run script calls the analysis using paths that will exist inside the container (e.g., '/app/data/...' if replication_data is mounted to /app/data). 2) Confirm container volume mapping: verify that host 'data/original/38/input/replication_data' is mounted to '/app/data' inside the container; then run inside container to check file presence. 3) Avoid relying on orchestrator cached plan; stop/restart container after changes to replication_info.json so plan is re-read.

1) Document and accept that the provided dataset differs from the original sample. If the goal is a direct reanalysis, attempt to obtain the original small-sample data or subsample the provided dataset to match original recruitment and sample size (if metadata allow). 2) If conducting a conceptual replication with the larger mixed-mode sample, pre-specify that this is a robustness check and report expected differences due to sample composition.

notes: Additional caveats and recommendations: (1) The provided dataset is an extended/combined dataset (1280 rows, ~320 participants) and will likely produce different SEs and possibly different effect magnitudes; treat any future estimates as conceptual replication/extension unless the original sample can be exactly matched. (2) Once code/path issues are fixed, run mixed-effects models and also compute cluster-robust SEs or participant-level clustering to be comparable with the within-subjects design. (3) Recommend renaming the CSV to remove spaces and/or use relative repository paths for outputs to avoid container/host mount mismatches. (4) After successful run, compare the losses coefficient sign, magnitude (mean difference in points), and p-value to original F(1,61)=4.73 (approx. -7.5 points difference reported). (5) If reproducibility in Docker is required, rebuild the Docker image only after editing replication_info.json and confirm the Dockerfile does not include syntax tokens like '(optional)' that break shell commands.
